title: Contribution of the Diffusion Potential to the Membrane Potential and to the Ion-Selective Electrode Response
abstract:
Contribution of the Diffusion Potential to the Membrane
Potential and to the Ion-Selective Electrode Response
Konstantin N. Mikhelson,혝* Andrzej Lewenstam,혝혝 and Svetlana E. Didina혝
혝 St. Petersburg University, Universitetskii pr.2, St. Petersburg, RUS-198904, Russia
혝혝Faculty of Material Science and Ceramics, University of Mining and Metallurgy, PL-30059, Cracow, Poland and Center for Process Analytical
Chemistry and Sensor Technology (Prosens), Abo Akademi University, FIN-20500, Turku, Finland
Received: November 12, 1998
Final version: February 22, 1999
Abstract
A generalized quasi-thermodynamic theory of the solvent polymeric ion-selective membranes containing ion-exchanging sites (charged
carrier) and a co-exchanger (ionic additives) and in contact with two mixed aqueous solutions of electrolytes IX2 and JX is developed. To
account for diffusion potential it is proposed to assume various pro짰les of species in the diffusion layer.
A computer simulation of the potentiometric signal and of its selectivity is ful짰lled. A surprising result of the simulations is that though the
value of diffusion potential achieves sometimes several tens of mV, it is almost the same for different pro짰les assumed. If being pro짰le-
independent, the diffusion potential occurs also time-independent. Therefore the usually fast response of ISEs actually does not mean that
only boundary potentials matter. The variability of selectivity coef짰cients to a large extent is also caused by diffusion potential.
Arti짰cial elimination of the diffusion potential with ionic additives to membranes based on calcium dioctylphenylphosphate allows one to
eliminate the peculiarities like Ca=pH potential dips and also to stabilize selectivity coef짰cients.
Keywords: Diffusion potentials in membranes, Selectivity coef짰cients, Boundary potential theory
1. Introduction
The overall electrical potentials across ion-selective membranes
are typically divided into two boundary potentials and diffusion
potential in the membrane. The question whether the diffusion
potential is signi짰cant or not remains unsolved. The fundamental
problem is that neither Galvanic potential at the membrane/solu-
tion interface, nor diffusion potential can be measured directly.
Therefore only indirect experimental indications on the relative
contribution from boundary potentials and that from diffusion
potential are available. It was shown recently that the theory
restricted to only boundary effects may be applied successfully to
describe how the potentiometric selectivity of electrodes depends
on the membrane composition [1짹5]. Indeed, most of the features
of ion-selective electrodes (ISEs) may be elucidated in a simple
manner by means of this model. Another fact which seem to be
against a signi짰cant contribution from the diffusion potential is the
well-known fact that the ISEs typically respond very fast, by far
faster than any steady pro짰le of species in the membrane may
establish. The fast response justi짰ed the choice of simpli짰cations
used in most of the theories which accounted both for boundary
and for diffusion potentials: those simpli짰cations were introduced
that made the diffusion potentials independent from the actual
pro짰le of species in the diffusion layer [6짹8].
On the other hand the signi짰cance of the various diffusion
phenomena for the ISEs response is also very well known [9].
The ability of ionophores to move in membranes is one of their
basic features. Membranes made of ion-exchange resins where
the charged sites are 짰xed do not show high selectivity. Correct
theoretical description of the anion interference with sodium-
selective ISEs requires the data of the species concentrations as
well as of relative mobilities of ion-ligand complexes and that of
co-extracted anions [10]. Recently it was proved that lower
detection limit of solvent polymeric ISEs is governed by diffu-
sion of ions across membranes. An appropriate choice of the
internal electrolyte may allow one to improve the detection limit
to a large extent [11, 12].
Numerical simulations of
ion-selective membranes with
charged sites in contact with mixed solutions of only monovalent
ions were carried out recently. It was shown that improvement of
membrane selectivity caused by ionic additives is to a great
extent due to elimination of diffusion potential. Lipophilic salt
formed by oppositely charged sites acts as a background elec-
trolyte in membranes and diffusion potential becomes negligible
[8].
The aim of this work is to evaluate the signi짰cance of diffusion
potential in systems with di- and monovalent ions by means of
numerical simulation and also to show clear (though indirect)
experimental evidence of how much it can contribute into the
measured EMF.
2. Theory
We consider a solvent polymeric membrane containing mobile
ion-exchanging sites R첼 and co-exchanger S혝. Accounting for
both charged and neutral carriers is also possible [7] but here for
simplicity we will consider membranes without neutral iono-
phores. Membranes are in contact with mixed solutions of
electrolytes IX2 혝 JX with divalent primary ions and monovalent
interference.
Boundary potential in the equilibrium state can be expressed

Concentration of free sites Cr is the value to be found to obtain
all other data. Thus species R첼 became a kind of ``key species''
de짰ning the whole situation in the membrane. Total concentra-
tions of exchanger sites R첼 and co-exchanger S혝 as well as other
parameters: ionic distribution coef짰cients ki and kj, formation
constants Kir, Kirr, Kjr and activities in aqueous solutions may be
considered as given values. Then it becomes possible to calculate
actual concentrations of all kind of species in the membrane and
also the boundary potential value.
To account for the diffusion potential we have to consider all
sorts of charge transfering species in the membrane: the coun-
terions I2+, J혝, the sites R첼 and S혝, as well as charged ion-pairs
IR혝. All kinds of species are characterized with their own
mobilities Ui, Uir, Uj, Ur, and Us. Diffusion potential in its dif-
ferential form will be:

Equation 6 can not be integrated rigorously without a knowledge

One of possible simpli짰cations to integrate Equation 6 is to
assume that the content of doubly charged species I2+ is negli-
gible compared with the others: Ci  Cir, Cirr. This assumption
was used earlier by Morf [6].
In this work a new approach is proposed: to assume various
pro짰les of ``key species'' concentration across the diffusion layer
in the membrane and to calculate a number of the corresponding
values of diffusion potential, and consequently a number of
membrane potential values. We have assumed linear Henderson
pro짰le and 짰ve different nonlinear pro짰les in the diffusion layer:
exponential increase (a), exponent with saturation (b), S-shaped
curve (c), sharp increase close to the external side (d), and sharp
increase close to the internal side of the diffusion layer (e), see
also Figure 1 and Equations. 7a짹e.

In these equations x is a dimensionless coordinate along the
diffusion layer with the thickness equal to 1. Pro짰les 7b and 7d
may seem reasonable for short times after change of the external
solution while Henderson linear pro짰le may establish in the
steady state [13]. Our aim was to see how much the calculated
diffusion potential will depend on the pro짰le. Wherefore we tried
also pro짰les 7a and 7e which may establish if there is a kind of
quasi interface within the membrane [14] and pro짰le 7c which
may be even irrelevant.
Each nonlinear curve was dissected into 1000 thin layers. A
linear pro짰le was assumed within each of these thin layers and
their contributions into the diffusion potential were calculated by
Henderson formalism and then summarized. Actually 100 sec-
tions soon give the same results as 1000 within 0.01 mV.
Therefore we can conclude that not only the number of sections
is enough but that the diffusion potential due to a nonlinear
pro짰le is equal to the sum of diffusion potentials of the linearized
sections.

both on their charge and on their mass. Therefore in simulation

exponential increase (a), exponent with saturation (b), S-shaped
curve (c), sharp increase close to the external side (d), and sharp
increase close to the internal side of the diffusion layer (e), see

linear pro짰le was assumed within each of these thin layers and
their contributions into the diffusion potential were calculated by
Henderson formalism and then summarized. Actually 100 sec-

Therefore we can conclude that not only the number of sections
is enough but that the diffusion potential due to a nonlinear
pro짰le is equal to the sum of diffusion potentials of the linearized
It was assumed that individual mobilities of species depend
both on their charge and on their mass. Therefore in simulation
Fig. 1. Concentration pro짰les in the diffusion layer of the membrane.
procedures we introduced mobilities of lipophilic ions Ur and Us
as parameters. Mobilities of smaller ions J혝 were assumed as
Uj 혞 3Us and Ui was assumed equal to 2Us if Us < Ur and 4Us if
Us > Ur. For ion pairs IR혝 it was assumed Uir 혞 Us.
Thus boundary potentials were calculated by Equation 1 while
diffusion contribution into the membrane potential was calculated
for seven different approximations. These were: neglecting dou-
bly charged species, assuming linear pro짰le for all of the species,
and assuming each of 짰ve different nonlinear pro짰les for a ``key-
species'', the last being free sites R첼.
The presented approach to the membrane electrical potential is
basically thermodynamical (irreversible thermodynamics with
respect to diffusion potential). It can be called quasithermody-
namical because we substitute activities of species within mem-
brane with the corresponding concentrations, and mobilities are
assumed constant. These are, of course, extrathermodynamical
assumptions, but very typical for ISEs theory [6].
Chemicals of pa grade were used. Calcium salt of dioctyl-
phenylphosphoric acid was synthesized in St. Petersburg Uni-
versity [15], tetradecylammonium salts were obtained from
Belarus University (Minsk, Belarus). Solvent-plasticizer tri(2-
ethylhexyl)phosphate was from Fluka AG (Buchs, Switzerland).
Poly(vinylchloride) (PVC) was from OHTALEN (St. Petersburg,
The membrane cocktails were prepared by dissolving appro-
priate amounts of ionophores, plasticizer, and PVC in freshly
distilled tetrahydrofuran. The cocktails were poured on the glass
plates and left to dry overnight to obtain membranes. The
membranes were glued to PVC bodies by a solution of PVC in
cyclohexanone. The electrodes were 짰lled with 0.01 M CaCl2.
The measurements of EMF were carried out at 20 1 C with
The electrodes and reference were connected to data acquisition
system HAN-3 (St.Petersburg, Russia) with high impedance
interface. The processed EMF values were averaged signals from
a 3 minutes collection period.
About 50 systems with various values of parameters were
simulated. Among them those seem especially interesting where
a nonmonotonous EMF signal is obtained. It is well known that
charged counterions the so-called ``potential dip'' [6, 16짹20] may
appear. According to [6] the dip appears in the systems with
strong association between a monovalent interference and
exchanging sites of low mobility. Therefore we simulated the
behavior of a membrane with Kir 혞 104, Kirr 혞 107, Kjr 혞 108,
Ksr 혞 1, and Us 혞 10Ur, containing Cr
M. High value of Kjr means high af짰nity of J혝 to R첼 like H혝
The curves corresponding to the whole membrane potential
Em, to the external boundary potential Eb, and to the diffusion
potential Ed simultaneously presented in Figure 2 elucidate the
origin of the potential dip. It can be seen that the dip appears
because of the trend of the diffusion potential to become more
negative with the increase of the interference activity in the
external aqueous solution. Negative sign of Ed is due to the
diffusion of R첼 from the membrane bulk which in turn is caused
by the strong association of JR producing the de짰cit of R첼 in the
Membrane highly loaded with co-exchanger S혝 provides no
dip, simply because Cr  Cs  constant at any value of aj and
thus diffusion potential is close to zero. Experimental data
obtained for calcium dioctylphenylphosphate based membranes
con짰rm this conclusion, see Figure 3. Membranes without
additives and with low amount of it produce the dip while those
highly doped with tetradecylammonium show a monotonous
change from calcium response to pH-response. Recently it was
shown that acidic ionophores in fact may act as neutral carriers at
low pH, producing the Ca/pH dip due to formation of mixed
complexes IJR2+ [21, 22]. It does not seem possible that addition
of tetradecylammonium may suppress formation of these mixed
complexes. Furthermore, some small but reproducible dips were
obtained when Cs혝 was an interference [19] and even for anion-
selective electrodes the analogous nonmonotonous curves were
reported [23]. Therefore the diffusion potential seems to be the
main cause of nonmonotonous response though formation of
complexes IJR2+ may also contribute into the effect.
The case of the potential dip where diffusion potential values
are indeed high gives the best example of how these values
depend on the assumed shape of the concentration pro짰le.
Results shown in Figure 4 demonstrate that though the values of
Ed themselves can reach tens of millivolts, the difference between
those corresponding to various approximations is surprisingly
small. Low dependence of Ed on the shape of the pro짰le means
that its time dependence should be low as well. Thus the exis-
tence of even high diffusion potentials in membranes does not
contradict with fast response of ISEs.
In other systems simulated it was assumed that Kirr, Kir > Kjr.
According to results of our calculations Ed in such membranes
did not exceed 15 mV. Therefore it does not cause here such
drastic effects like a nonmonotonous response but contributes
into the variability of selectivity coef짰cients. Arti짰cial elimina-
tion of the diffusion potential by ionic additives may therefore
make selectivity coef짰cients constant.
Fig. 2. Effect of the activity of monovalent interference J혝 on the elec-
trical potentials: Em, Eb, and Ed. Parameter values are: ki 혞 10첼5,
tot. The monotonous curve Em,10
corresponds to the membrane with Cs
Diffusion Potential Contributions
Potentiometric selectivity was calculated treating the simulated
membrane potentials according to 3 different equations, recom-
The latter Equation 10 was proposed 짰rst by Morf [6] for ion-
exchanging membranes and more recently rederived in a new
manner by Bakker and coauthors for various kinds of membranes
[2]. Results calculated for membrane highly loaded with co-
exchanger are presented in Figure 5. As can be seen, Kij
IUPAC are far from being constants. As to the value of
Morf, it remains constant in the whole range of ai thus being
Calibrations of electrodes with membranes based on calcium
dioctylphenylphosphate in mixed solutions of CaCl2 in the pre-
sence of highly interfering tetrabutylammonium cations are given
on Figure 6. Advantage to use highly interfering ion is that it
makes possible to vary also the background of the interference
and to avoid artifacts. The latter are due to contamination of
samples caused by primary ions from membranes [26].
One can see clearly how the selectivity became improved with
addition of TDA혝 to the membranes. Experimental values of
selectivity coef짰cients calculated by means of Equations (8짹10)
depend both on aCa and on aTBA in aqueous solutions. But in
case of the membrane with 50 % of TDA혝 added to Ca(DOPP)2
see Figure 7. Only when both primary and interfering ions are

of ions from aqueous solution into the membrane): curve 3; other non-

contained 0.05 M Ca(DOPP)2 and tetradecylammonium as co-exchanger: 0, 10, and 50 % relative to Ca(DOPP)2.

present at low concentration the interference became over-
The most striking result of numerical simulations is that dif-
fusion potentials in membranes do not depend much on the actual
pro짰le of species concentrations. Therefore their values establish
fast, long before the steady pro짰les do. Consequently ISEs with
high values of Ed still may respond fast. Diffusion potentials are
especially high in cases when the sites are more selective to
interference than to primary ions, like in Ca=pH case. In most
other cases diffusion potentials do not exceed 15 mV. Therefore
the boundary potential theory may be enough to guide how to
improve the selectivity, but ISEs do not obey simple equations
quantitatively. Ionic additives in membranes allow one to improve
the selectivity. Elimination of the diffusion potential (together
with stabilization of free sites concentration and, probably,

Investigation of interference from highly discriminated ions
like potassium will be the topic of our future article.
